_comment: Enrichment data (anvaya + literal_meaning + traditional_meaning_consensus + disagreements) for verses 6.25-6.47, where Sanskrit + verbatim translations + commentary excerpts are already captured in the existing source packs but the analytical fields are blank. This file populates those fields in a single builder pass.

25.literal: Gradually, gradually, by intellect held with conviction, one should arrest the mind; having made the mind established in the self, one should not think of anything at all.
25.traditional: Krishna gives the technique. Gradually (śanaiḥ śanaiḥ — emphatically reduplicated for slow patient progression), by buddhi (intellect) held by dhṛti (conviction/firmness), one should arrest (uparamet) the mind. Having made the mind ātma-saṁstha (established in the self), one should not think of anything at all (na kiñcid api cintayet). The verse is the operational sequel to 6.24's renunciation: 6.24 is the precondition; 6.25 is the active practice. Shankara reads ātma-saṁstha as the final position; Ramanuja reads it operationally as the discipline.

positions.rendering: established in the (Advaita) Self / brahman
established on the discriminating self / inner self with the divine as object
fixed on the Self alone

disagreements.explanation: All three traditions agree on the operational instruction; the Advaita and Vishishtadvaita readings of what 'ātman' refers to differ as expected, but the practical instruction (mind held one-pointedly inward) is identical across translators.

26.literal: From wherever the unsteady, restless mind wanders out — from there, having restrained it, one should bring it back under the control of the self.
26.traditional: Krishna names the maintenance instruction. The mind is conceded to be cañcala (restless) and asthira (unsteady) — the doctrine that becomes Arjuna's question at 6.33-6.34. The instruction: from whatever direction the mind wanders out (yato yataḥ), from there (tatas tataḥ) restrain it and bring it back under the self's control. The verse is operational; both Shankara and Ramanuja read it as a practice-instruction for the actual minute-by-minute work of meditation.

positions.rendering: in the Self / under the self
under one's own discriminating control
under the control of the Self

disagreements.explanation: Translators agree this is the inner discipline of attention-return; the philosophical scope of ātman is preserved.

27.literal: For the yogi of peaceful mind, whose passion is calmed, who has become brahman, free of stain — supreme happiness comes.
27.traditional: The verse names the result of sustained practice. The yogi of praśānta-manas (peaceful mind), in whom rajas (the passion-mode) is śānta (calmed), who has become brahma-bhūta (brahman-realized) and akalmaṣa (free of stain), receives uttamam sukham (supreme happiness). Shankara reads brahma-bhūta as the Advaita identity-with-brahman; Ramanuja reads it as the state of the liberated practitioner approaching the divine.

positions.rendering: identical with brahman
approaching the brahman-state, the liberated soul
on the spiritual platform

disagreements.explanation: Classic Advaita/Vishishtadvaita divergence on the meaning of brahma-bhūta (identity vs. similarity-of-state).

28.literal: Yoking the self thus continually, the yogi free from stain easily attains supreme happiness — touch with brahman.
28.traditional: Krishna names the easy attainment. The yogi who continually (sadā) yokes the self, become free from stain (vigata-kalmaṣa), easily (sukhena) reaches the touch of brahman (brahma-saṁsparśa) — boundless happiness (atyantam sukham). 'Easily' is striking: the verse names the discipline as initially difficult but, once established, the supreme reach is sukhena, easily. The discipline-makes-the-discipline-easy claim.

positions.rendering: identity-touch with brahman (Advaita)
contact / direct experience of brahman
transcendental loving service of the Lord

disagreements.explanation: Sectarian reading of saṁsparśa (touch / contact / loving union); Gaudiya tradition reads it through the bhakti lens.

29.literal: The one whose self is yoked in yoga, with equal vision in all directions, sees the self abiding in all beings, and all beings in the self.
29.traditional: Krishna introduces the sama-darśana doctrine that culminates the meditation block. The yoga-yukta-ātmā (one whose self is yoked in yoga) sees the self as resident in all beings and all beings in the self. Shankara reads this as the Advaita realization of identity; Ramanuja reads it as the Vishishtadvaita recognition that all beings have the same nature as eternal selves under the same Lord; Prabhupada reads it through the Gaudiya lens of seeing all beings as parts of the Lord.
anvaya: one whose self is yoked in yoga

positions.rendering: the same Self abiding in all beings (Advaita identity)
the self as one species across all beings, with each preserving individual selfhood under the Lord
the Supersoul / Paramātmā present in every being

disagreements.explanation: Major sectarian divergence on what sarva-bhūta-stham means; Advaita identity-claim vs Vishishtadvaita commonality-of-class vs Gaudiya Paramātmā-presence.

30.literal: One who sees me everywhere, and all in me — I am not lost to that one, and that one is not lost to me.
30.traditional: Krishna shifts to the first-person (mām, mayi). The verse introduces explicit theistic language. One who sees Krishna everywhere and all in Krishna is the inverse — Krishna is not lost (na praṇaśyāmi) to that one, and that one is not lost to Krishna. Strong devotional verse; Ramanuja and Prabhupada both read it explicitly theistically; Shankara reads māṁ as 'the Supreme Self' which the verse equates with brahman.

positions.rendering: the supreme Self / brahman in personal mode
the Lord as the inner ruler of all
the Supreme Personality of Godhead, Krishna

disagreements.explanation: Theistic divergence: Advaita reads māṁ as a personal mode of brahman; Vishishtadvaita and Gaudiya read it as the personal Lord.

31.literal: The yogi who, established in oneness, worships me as abiding in all beings — though acting in all ways, that yogi abides in me.
31.traditional: Krishna intensifies the previous verse. The yogi who is ekatvam-āsthitaḥ (established in oneness) — that is, in the unity-vision of 6.29 — and who worships Krishna as abiding-in-all-beings, that yogi, however acting (sarvathā vartamāno 'pi), abides in Krishna. The phrase 'however acting' is doctrinally sharp: the abiding does not require special actions; it persists across whatever activity. Ramanuja reads ekatvam as oneness-of-class-of-souls under the Lord; Shankara as identity.

positions.rendering: oneness as Advaita identity with brahman
unity-of-essence among all selves under the supreme Self
oneness as the conscious knowing of the Lord present in all

disagreements.explanation: Identity-vs-similarity-of-essence reading; the same surface word, the deepest sectarian divergence in chapter 6.

32.literal: The yogi who, by analogy with the self, sees alike everywhere — whether in pleasure or pain — that yogi is held to be supreme, O Arjuna.
32.traditional: Krishna gives the operational test of sama-darśana. The yogi who, ātma-aupamyena (by analogy with the self), sees equally everywhere — applying the same response-shape to others' pleasure-or-pain as to one's own — is paramaḥ (supreme). The verse closes the meditation block (6.10-6.32) with the empathy-doctrine; Ramanuja emphasises that the verse does not endorse ignoring others' pain but seeing it with the same urgency one feels for one's own.

positions.rendering: sees the same Self / same nature in others' pain
applies the same urgency of response to others' pain as to one's own
sees the equal happiness and distress of all by comparing them with himself

disagreements.explanation: Operationally the readings agree; metaphysically they differ as expected.

33.literal: Arjuna said: O Madhusūdana, this yoga of equality you have spoken of — I do not see its firm continuation, on account of restlessness.
33.traditional: The chapter's dialectical break. Arjuna names the difficulty after the long meditation-block: this yoga of equality (sāmyena) you have spoken — I do not see its sthirām sthitim (firm/permanent establishment); cañcalatvāt — because of restlessness (the restlessness of the mind). The interruption is honest; the chapter has been giving meditation-instruction for 23 verses, and Arjuna has reached the limit of his ability to imagine sustaining it. Shankara and Ramanuja both read this as a sincere question, not a hostile one.

34.literal: For the mind is restless, churning, strong, obstinate, O Krishna; restraining it I think is as hard as restraining the wind.
34.traditional: Arjuna intensifies. He gives four adjectives for the mind: cañcala (restless), pramāthi (churning, agitating), balavat (strong), dṛḍha (firm/obstinate). Restraining it (nigraham), he thinks, is as hard as restraining the wind. The wind-comparison is the chapter's most quoted simile from Arjuna's side. Both Shankara and Ramanuja read this as a sincere admission, not a complaint; the practitioner is naming what the practice actually feels like.

35.literal: The Blessed Lord said: Without doubt, mighty-armed one, the mind is hard to restrain and restless. But by practice (abhyāsa) and dispassion (vairāgya), O son of Kuntī, it is grasped.
35.traditional: Krishna's answer — and the chapter's operational answer to Arjuna's objection. He concedes the difficulty: asaṁśayam (without doubt), the mind is durnigraha (hard to restrain) and cala (restless). But — and this is the chapter's central practical instruction — by abhyāsa (sustained practice) and vairāgya (dispassion), it is gṛhyate (grasped, brought under control). The two-handed answer is doctrinal: practice alone is not enough; dispassion alone is not enough; both together are required. The same pair appears in Yoga Sūtras 1.12 (abhyāsa-vairāgyābhyāṁ tan-nirodhaḥ).

positions.rendering: repeated effort of fixing the mind on a single object
sustained engagement of the mind on the Self
steady practice of remembering the Supreme Lord

disagreements.explanation: All translators agree on practice + dispassion as the answer; the object of practice differs by tradition.

36.literal: For one whose self is unrestrained, yoga is hard to obtain — that is my view. But for one whose self is disciplined, striving with proper means, it is attainable.
36.traditional: Krishna closes Arjuna's question. For the asaṁyat-ātmā (unrestrained-self), yoga is duṣprāpa (hard to obtain). But for the vaśya-ātmā (disciplined-self) yatatā (striving) upāyataḥ (with proper means), it is śakyaḥ avāptum (capable of being attained). The verse names the precondition: the practice presumes the disciplinary work has begun. It is not promised to those who have not started; it is promised to those who are striving with the right means.
anvaya: by one whose self is unrestrained
by one whose self is disciplined

37.literal: Arjuna said: The one without self-restraint though endowed with faith, whose mind has fallen from yoga, not having attained the perfection of yoga — what path does that one go to, O Krishna?
37.traditional: Arjuna's pivot to the consolation question. The hypothetical: ayatiḥ (without restraint) but śraddhayā upetaḥ (faith-endowed); mind fallen from yoga (yogāc calita-mānasaḥ); having not attained yoga-saṁsiddhi (the perfection of yoga). What gati (path) does that one go? The question opens the chapter's emotional center — the practitioner who has tried, has had faith, has lost the practice along the way. Shankara and Ramanuja both read Arjuna as asking sincerely for the practitioner who fears having failed.

38.literal: Fallen from both, O mighty-armed one, established in nothing, deluded on the path of brahman — does he not perish like a torn cloud?
38.traditional: Arjuna intensifies the question. Ubhaya-vibhraṣṭa (fallen from both) — that is, fallen from both worldly results and yogic results; apratiṣṭha (established in nothing); vimūḍha (deluded) on the brahmaṇaḥ pathi (path of brahman). The simile chinnābhram iva (like a torn cloud) — does that one not perish, dispersing into nothing? The verse's emotional weight comes from the simile: the cloud that has neither rejoined another cloud nor reached the rain — torn between, dispersed. Shankara and Ramanuja read this as Arjuna's most acute fear for the half-finished practitioner.

39.literal: This doubt of mine, O Krishna, you alone are competent to cut entirely; for there is no other than you who could be the cutter of this doubt.
39.traditional: Arjuna's plea. The doubt (saṁśaya) is to be cut (chettum) entirely (aśeṣataḥ). Only Krishna can do it — no other than him is upapadyate (able). The verse is structurally significant: Arjuna names Krishna's authority to answer, granting the question to him alone. Shankara reads this as the student's recognition of the teacher's unique competence; Ramanuja reads it as the devotional appeal to the Lord.

40.literal: The Blessed Lord said: O Pārtha, neither here nor in the next world is there destruction for that one. For no doer of good, my friend, comes to a bad end.
40.traditional: Krishna's answer — and the chapter's emotional center. The fallen yogi is not destroyed (vināśa); not in this world (iha), not in the next (amutra). The reason: na hi kalyāṇa-kṛt kaścit durgatiṁ tāta gacchati — no doer of good (kalyāṇa-kṛt) ever, my friend (tāta), goes to a bad end (durgatim). The address tāta is unusually warm — the teacher addresses the student affectionately. Shankara, Ramanuja, and Prabhupada all read this as the chapter's most tender moment.

41.literal: Having reached the worlds of those who do meritorious work, having dwelt there for endless years, the one fallen from yoga is born in the home of the pure and prosperous.
41.traditional: Krishna names the first of two paths for the fallen yogi. After reaching puṇya-kṛtām lokān (worlds of the meritorious) and dwelling there śāśvatīḥ samāḥ (endless years — read variously as 'many years' or 'eons'), the yoga-bhraṣṭa (one fallen from yoga) is reborn in the home of the śucī (pure) and śrīmat (prosperous). The verse names the lower of the two consolatory paths.

positions.rendering: endless years (relatively, until karma exhausts)
many years, in the world of the meritorious
many, many years (in the planets of the pious living entities)

disagreements.explanation: All three read this as an extended but bounded duration; the word śāśvatī ('eternal/endless') is read relatively in the sense of 'very long'.

42.literal: Or one is born in the family of yogis themselves, who are wise — such birth is harder still to obtain in this world.
42.traditional: The second path. Atha vā (or) — the alternative — being born in the family of yogis themselves (yoginām eva kule), who are dhīmatām (wise). Krishna explicitly says this is durlabhataram (rarer still). The verse implies a hierarchy: the lower consolation is rebirth into prosperous purity; the higher is rebirth into the lineage of practitioners themselves. Both Shankara and Ramanuja read this as a real promise about birth-conditions; Prabhupada reads it explicitly as material rebirth into devotional families.

43.literal: There that one regains the union of intellect from the prior body, and from there strives further toward perfection, O joy of the Kurus.
43.traditional: Krishna names the operational consequence. Tatra (there, in the new birth) the practitioner labhate (gains, regains) the buddhi-saṁyoga (the union of intellect / yoga of intellect) paurva-dehika (from the prior body). From there (tato bhūyaḥ) strives further (yatate) toward saṁsiddhi (perfection). The verse is the chapter's most explicit claim about cross-life continuity of practice. Both Shankara and Ramanuja read this as a real claim about the trans-life carrying-forward of yogic discipline.

44.literal: By that very prior practice, that one is carried — even involuntarily. Even the one who merely wishes to know yoga goes beyond the brahman-of-sound (ritual injunction).
44.traditional: Krishna deepens the consolation. Pūrvābhyāsena tena eva — by that very prior practice — the practitioner hriyate (is carried) even avaśaḥ api (even unwilling). The compound force is striking: the prior practice itself drags the practitioner forward, regardless of the present-life will. Even one who is merely jijñāsuḥ (desirous of knowing) yoga goes beyond śabda-brahma (the brahman of sound, that is, mere Vedic ritual injunction). Shankara reads śabda-brahma as the karma-kāṇḍa portion of the Vedas; Ramanuja reads it similarly.

positions.rendering: the brahman of sound, i.e., the karma-kāṇḍa Vedic ritual portion
the Veda taken merely as ritual injunction
ritualistic principles of the scriptures

disagreements.explanation: Translators agree śabda-brahma here refers to mere ritual Vedic action that the yoga-practitioner transcends; the philosophical scope of brahman as logos is preserved at the higher reading.

45.literal: But the yogi striving with effort, purified of stain, perfected through many births — from that one reaches the supreme goal.
45.traditional: Krishna names the final result. The yogi prayatnād yatamānaḥ (striving with effort), saṁśuddha-kilbiṣaḥ (purified of all stain), aneka-janma-saṁsiddhaḥ (perfected across many births) — that one reaches the parām gatim (supreme goal). The chapter's most explicit claim that practice compounds across lives. The reading sequence 6.40-6.45 is the consolation passage: not destroyed (40), reborn in good circumstances (41-42), regains prior practice (43), carried by it even unwilling (44), reaches the supreme goal across many lives (45).

46.literal: The yogi is greater than the ascetic; greater even than the knower (jñānī); greater than the doer of works (karmin); therefore, O Arjuna, be a yogi.
46.traditional: Krishna closes the chapter with a hierarchy. The yogi is adhika (greater) than three other classes: the tapasvin (ascetic), the jñānin (knower), the karmin (ritualist / doer of works). Therefore (tasmāt), be a yogī. Shankara reads this as Krishna's commendation of the integrated yogi over each of the partial paths; Ramanuja reads jñānin here as one who has merely studied scriptures, vs. the yogi who has lived the practice; the hierarchy is held across all three traditional commentaries.

47.literal: Of all yogis, the one who, with inner self gone to me, faithful, worships me — that one is most yoked of all, in my view.
47.traditional: The chapter's closing verse and theistic close. Of all yogis, the one with inner self gone to Krishna (mad-gatena antar-ātmanā), endowed with faith (śraddhāvān), who worships Krishna (bhajate māṁ) — that one is yukta-tamaḥ (most yoked of all), in Krishna's view. The verse is explicitly devotional and anticipates the bhakti emphasis of chapter 12. Shankara reads it as completing the yoga-hierarchy with the bhakti-yogī as supreme; Ramanuja reads it as the chapter's natural culmination in devotion to the Lord.

positions.rendering: contemplates / meditates on / serves
renders devotional service / worships
engages in My loving devotional service

disagreements.explanation: Sectarian divergence on whether bhajate is Advaita-style contemplation or Vishishtadvaita/Gaudiya-style devotional service.
